data.title: Pakistani leader working toward 'conducive environment' for US-Iran peace talks
data.description:
The Pakistani government said Saturday that the prime minister is working to “create a conducive environment” for peace negotiations between the U.S. and Iran as the conflict reaches the one-month mark.

Iranian President Masoud Pezeshkian and Pakistani Prime Minister Muhammad Shehbaz Sharif discussed ongoing hostilities in the region and Pakistan’s outreach efforts with the U.S. and neighboring Gulf states in a phone call, according to the prime minister’s office.

“He briefed the Iranian President on the strong endorsement of Pakistan’s peace initiative and expressed the hope that a viable path towards ending hostilities could be found collectively,” the office said in a statement posted on the social platform X, adding that the call lasted over an hour.

The office said Pezeshkian praised Sharif’s diplomatic effort to facilitate talks and mediation in a phone call, but told him that trust was needed to move forward.  

“Attacks on infrastructure and assassinations by aggressors show they cannot be trusted,” Pezeshkian said, according to an account from the Iranian government.

The Iranian government said Pezeshkian declared that his country’s “defensive operation is natural” and expressed that Tehran “views Muslim countries as brothers and seeks no harm to any Muslim.”

The call comes as tensions in the Middle East continue to escalate, with Iran vowing to retaliate against U.S. and Israeli interests following strikes on two nuclear facilities and other Iranian targets.

Iranian Foreign Minister Abbas Araghchi said on Friday that his country would “exact HEAVY price” for the Israeli attacks, which he claimed violated a 10-day pause issued by President Trump.

“Israel has hit 2 of Iran’s largest steel factories, a power plant and civilian nuclear sites among other infrastructure. Israel claims it acted in coordination with the U.S.,” Araghchi wrote on X. 

That same day, 10 U.S. servicemembers were injured in a reported Iranian missile attack on Prime Sultan Air Base in Saudi Arabia. The missile damaged several U.S. refueling craft and unmanned aerial vehicles, The Wall Street Journal reported, citing U.S. officials.

Fears of wider conflict intensified on Saturday as Iran-backed Houthis in Yemen claimed responsibility for an early morning missile attack on Israel, which the Israeli government said was intercepted without casualties, according to the Associated Press. 

Trump and Secretary of State Marco Rubio touted progress this week in negotiations with Tehran to end the war that began on Feb. 28 following failed talks over the Islamic Republic’s nuclear program.

Iran has denied its involvement in talks with the Trump administration, and publicly rejected a 15-point ceasefire proposal from the U.S. sent via Pakistani mediators.

The country has instead issued its own set of demands, which include control over the Strait of Hormuz, an end to U.S.-Israeli “aggression” and reparations, among others.

data.title: Exiled Iranian crown prince tells CPAC that 'a free Iran is not a fantasy'
data.description:
Reza Pahlavi, the exiled crown prince of Iran, said freedom in Iran is “within reach” in a speech at the Conservative Political Action Conference (CPAC) on Saturday. 

“A free Iran is not a fantasy,” Pahlavi said. “A free Iran is within reach right now, but as we all know, freedom never comes free.”

The son of Iran’s last shah made the comments to the crowd of conservatives in Grapevine, Texas, amid intensifying military action in the Middle East. On Saturday, the Iranian-backed Houthis joined the fight, launching strikes on Israel, which said the country intercepted the attack without casualty. 

Pahlavi encouraged the U.S. to “stay the course” in its military operations against Tehran.

“Do not throw this crumbling regime a lifeline,” he said. “Pave the way for the Iranian people to finish the job.”

President Trump said on Thursday that he would extend a pause on striking Iranian energy infrastructure. However, Iranian Foreign Minister Abbas Araghchi accused Israel of violating this agreement in strikes on Iran’s two largest steel plants the following day. 

Araghchi vowed to “exact HEAVY price” against joint Israeli-U.S. military operations. 

Pahlavi reupped his pitch in his Saturday speech to run Iran in the interim as the country sets up a democratic election process to select its next leader. 

“Millions of Iranians have called on me to lead the transition to democracy. I have accepted that call not to serve myself, but to serve my nation and my people,” Pahlavi said, eliciting loud applause from the conservative crowd. 

After U.S. strikes killed Iranian Supreme Leader Ayatollah Ali Khamenei in February, a body of clerics appointed his son, Mojtaba Khamenei, to replace him. The younger Khamenei was also reportedly injured in the strikes that killed his father, mother, wife and son. 

The exiled prince said “life can never go back to normal” in Iran after the regime’s bloody crackdown on protests in the country. Protesters demonstrated against the Iranian regime following a dramatic collapse in the country’s currency at the end of last year. 

An activist group said at the end of January that over 6,000 people were killed during the regime’s military response, including 5,804 demonstrators, 214 government-affiliated forces, 92 children and 49 civilians who were not participating in the protests. 

“There is a sea of blood between the people and the regime,” Pahlavi said. “After all the massacres, after all they have sacrificed, they will never agree to swap one tyrant for another.”

The exiled royal praised Trump’s military attacks against Tehran and said, the “Iranian story is not yet finished.”

“Great civilizations outlast even the most vicious occupiers,” he told the crowd. “With your help and with the courage, sacrifice and heroism of Iran’s greatest youth, our best, latest chapter is being written right now. 

“When it is done, a free and democratic Iran will stand alongside the United States as a partner, an ally and a friend,” he continued. “President Trump is making America great again. I intend to make Iran great again.”

data.title: How MAGA divides over Iran manifested at CPAC
data.description:
The annual Conservative Political Action Conference in Texas has underscored persistent divides within the Republican Party over President Trump’s military campaign in Iran. 

The four-day conference, which ends Saturday in Grapevine, has offered insights into how party leaders are balancing their support for Trump’s “America First” priorities amid growing concern among voters about the conflict’s impact on the economy. 

Although many speakers noted their ultimate confidence in the president, who didn’t attend the event for the first time in a decade, a handful of speakers and longtime Trump allies voiced concerns over U.S. military operations in the Middle East.

Former Rep. Matt Gaetz (R-Fla.) cautioned the president against sending U.S. troops to Iran and warned that a ground invasion could negatively affect the economy.

“I want President Trump to have every diplomatic tool at his disposal, and I do trust that he knows a great deal more than I do,” Gaetz, who was Trump’s initial pick to lead the Department of Justice, said in a Thursday speech.

“But a ground invasion of Iran will make our country poorer and less safe,” he continued. “It will mean higher gas prices, higher food prices, and I’m not sure we’d end up killing more terrorists than we would create.”

Gas prices, since the onset of the conflict in late February, have surged by more than $1 a gallon. The administration has argued that this is a short-term phenomenon, seeking to downplay consumer woes over high prices at the pump.

“I thought the oil prices would go up more, and I thought the stock market would go down more,” the president said Thursday. “It hasn’t been nearly as severe as I thought.”

Meanwhile, former White House strategist and conservative media personality Steve Bannon warned of the possibility of ground troops in the Middle Eastern country, but also stopped short of directly criticizing Trump. 

“You have to be convinced that this is the right thing to do, particularly now that we’re on the eve of potentially the insertion of American combat troops,” Bannon told the CPAC crowd on Friday. “Your sons, daughters, granddaughters, grandsons, could be on Kharg Island or be holding a beachhead down by the Strait of Hormuz.”

Gaetz and Bannon are part of a growing number of conservatives in Trump’s base who have expressed some skepticism over the monthlong campaign in Iran.

A number of speakers at CPAC, however, defended Trump and his actions in the region. 

“This did not happen by accident,” Reza Pahlavi, an exiled crown prince from Iran, said Saturday. ”It happened because of President Trump’s resolve and the courage of American troops on behalf.” 

The White House has indicated in recent days that the U.S. is winding down its operations in Iran, with press secretary Karoline Leavitt stating the military’s “core objectives” have been met. 

Still, 6 in 10 Americans disapprove of U.S. actions in Iran, according to the Pew Research Center. Forty-four percent strongly disapproved and 17 percent somewhat disapproved of the conflict. Republican voters, however, were more likely to support Trump’s actions in the region. Sixty-nine percent of GOP respondents mostly favored the conflict, per the survey. 

But that’s not to say there is overwhelming support of the military campaign across the entire conservative base. Generational divides over the conflict were particularly noticeable at CPAC this weekend, per The Associated Press.

“We did not want to see more wars. We wanted actual ‘America First’ policies, and Trump was very explicit about that,” said Benjamin Williams, a 25-year-old marketing specialist for Young Americans for Liberty, AP reported. “It does feel like a betrayal, for sure.”

Older CPAC attendees were less likely to criticize the president over military action than younger conservatives. Older party members argued that Trump was simply responding to an active threat. 

“I don’t believe he started a new war. He was acting in response to a 40-year-old war by Iran,” said 70-year-old retired defense contractor Joe Ropar of McKinney, Texas, told the AP. “How long were we supposed to wait? I think he did what he had to do when he had to do it.”

data.title: What would it take for Republicans to win California's gubernatorial race?
data.description:
California’s 2026 gubernatorial primary poses an unusual question: Can Republicans exploit the state’s top-two system to lock out Democrats, but only by making themselves seemingly less electable for the general election? 

Let me explain: With Democrats fractured across a crowded field, there is a plausible path for the two leading Republican contenders, Chad Bianco and Steve Hilton, to place first and second. This would exclude all Democratic candidates from the general-election ballot. California’s jungle primary — with dozens of candidates set to appear on the June 2 ballot — offers Republicans this rare opening.

Even so, with Democrats setting April 14 (or so I hear) as the deadline for low-polling candidates to withdraw, the Republicans’ deeper problem remains unchanged. To win statewide, they would still need a candidate with genuine crossover appeal. Yet the kind of candidate most likely to survive a Republican consolidation fight is also probably the kind least likely to win in California.

Registered Democrats outnumber Republicans in California by almost two-to-one. California Republicans have at times come closer than expected in statewide races. In the 2022 controller’s race, Republicans came within 11 points. But with only about 25 percent of registered voters, Republicans need near-total base turnout just to remain competitive for a top-two finish. 

That dynamic pressures the party’s two leading candidates to play to a shrinking and increasingly ideological Republican electorate. Bianco, amid slipping polls, just seized 650,000 election ballots from 2025, ostensibly to curry favor with President Trump and the Trumpiest of voters in California. Hilton, for his part, has attacked Bianco’s MAGA credentials by hammering him for allegedly taking a knee during a 2020 Black Lives Matter protest. In the race’s first debate, Hilton brushed aside the opening question to aggressively attack Bianco and urge Republican voters to consolidate behind him.

That is the central paradox of the race. Does any Republican with a plausible path through the primary now have to become, or at least present himself as, a MAGA candidate? If so, the party has constructed a mechanism that screens out the very sort of nominee who might actually compete statewide.

Bianco and Hilton are not irrational actors, oblivious to California’s deep-blue reality. Nor are their appeals to MAGA voters entirely insincere. Both men plainly understand the incentives of the modern Republican electorate and, to varying degrees, are authentically aligned with it.

But they are responding rationally to the structure in front of them in order to secure the nomination — notwithstanding the fact that both candidates’ campaigns are fundamentally rooted in more than Trump-era cultural signaling, offering substantive proposals to solve California’s problems of homelessness, crime and the budget.

Many of those proposals are populist: both Hilton and a progressive Democrat, Katie Porter, are running for governor on the proposal to eliminate state income taxes for families making under $100,000.

But in a general election, both Republicans would likely be badly constrained if, as they have suggested, they seek Trump’s endorsement in order to consolidate the Republican vote and survive June. The strategy that may help them advance in June could all but foreclose their chances in November. 

The strong opening for the California Republican Party is not simply for a Republican as such, but for a post-partisan executive: a socially moderate, managerial, tough-on-crime, tough-on-homelessness candidate in the mold of for former Gov. Arnold Schwarzenegger, someone capable of appealing to suburban voters without completely alienating the remaining GOP base.

Many Californians, including some Democrats and independents, are plainly frustrated with the state’s one-party dominance and the political culture that produced Gov. Gavin Newsom (D) and former Vice President Kamala Harris. Against an establishment Democrat like Rep. Eric Swalwell (D) — who would read, fairly or not, as more of the same — such a Republican might have a genuine opening, even if the primary does not produce a lockout.

Even then, such a candidate would still have to prove more compelling than left-populists like Tom Steyer and Porter, who are channeling the Bernie Sanders wing on affordability and progressive economic reform, and who may present the toughest general-election challenge of all.

There is, admittedly, a faintly comic version of this story in which Steve Hilton, a British import and consummate outsider, wins the primary, abruptly renounces MAGA, and assembles the sort of cross-partisan coalition that could win in California — whether that means sticking to populist rhetoric or abandoning it in hopes of getting some Romney Republicans on board. But political reinventions of that sort are rare and fewer still succeed. Republicans can dream. Whether they can California dream is another matter.

William Liang is a writer living in San Francisco.

data.title: U.S. sailors, Marines deployed amid Iran war reach Centcom region
data.description:
U.S. sailors and Marines aboard the USS Tripoli arrived at the U.S. Central Command (Centcom) area in the Middle East on Friday.

Centcom stated Saturday that the flagship delivered about 3,500 sailors and Marines from the Tripoli Amphibious Ready Group of the 31st Marine Expeditionary Unit, along with “transport and strike fighter aircraft, as well as amphibious assault and tactical assets.”

The Pentagon last week reportedly sped up the deployment of thousands more Marines and two warships to the region. The San Diego-based USS Boxer, carrying at least 2,200 Marines with the 11th Marine Expeditionary Unit, departed Wednesday and is less than a month away from reaching the Centcom area.

Both ships will be joined by the USS New Orleans, which was scheduled to arrive Friday in the region.

The military buildup in the Middle East comes amid calls for President Trump to not deploy boots on the ground as part of “Operation Epic Fury.” Trump has said he does not intend to send troops to fight, though he has floated the idea of taking over Iran’s crucial crude export storage depot, a tactic that would require U.S. ground troops.

The Houthis, a Yemen-based armed group that backs Iran’s Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps, fired ballistic missiles at Israel on Saturday, Al Jazeera reported, citing Houthi spokesperson Brig. Gen. Yahya Saree. The Israeli Defense Forces said they intercepted a missile from Yemen. No injuries and damage have been reported.

The U.S.-designated foreign terrorist organization on Friday warned the U.S., Israel and Persian Gulf partners against further hostilities toward Iran. It called for a diplomatic conclusion to the conflict and warned against any escalation of hostilities.

Secretary of State Marco Rubio said Friday that the U.S. was still working on negotiations with Iran through Pakistani mediators to end the conflict in “weeks, not months,” he told The Guardian.

Iran rejected the U.S.’s 15-point peace plan and submitted a counterproposal requesting an end to hostilities and sovereignty over the Strait of Hormuz, among other provisions. The strait has been closed since the conflict began, causing oil and gas prices to skyrocket.

Trump on Thursday extended his deadline for Iran to reopen the Strait of Hormuz on Friday to April 6 after saying talks with Iran were “going very well.”

data.title: 2028 is the Republican Party's second chance
data.description:
There has been a lot of discussion recently about the future of the conservative movement. To anti-MAGA principled conservatives and legacy Republicans who are feeling lost, confused or dispirited — and there are many — I remind you of one immutable fact: There will be an election for president in 34 months. According to the 22nd Amendment of the Constitution, President Trump is forbidden from being elected more than twice. That changes everything.

No matter how often Trump trolls about third terms or cancelled elections, he can’t change the calendar or the Constitution. For the first time since Spring 2016, there will be a contest for control of the Republican party and the direction of the conservative movement.

Elections matter. In fact, in American politics, they are the only thing that matters. This has been painfully obvious for the last 14 months of the second Trump administration. Trump and his MAGA movement were able to capture the Republican party and turn it away from Reaganite principles because he won the nomination and the general election in 2016. 

Some would argue that the party’s base had been edging towards populism for some time. Perhaps. But remember, the Republican Party nominated Mitt Romney in 2012. And does anyone doubt that the party would look very different today if someone other than Trump had won our nomination in 2016?

We are confident that, at heart, millions of Republicans still support the Constitution and the rule of law and want a government that will unite rather than divide Americans. They still support free enterprise and free trade, fiscal responsibility and a reduction in our national debt. And they still believe in peace through strength and want America to stand by its traditional allies, Ukraine, NATO, Denmark and our other longtime friends around the world.

We know that next year, as candidates for president come forward, one or more of those candidates will stand for these values and will work to redeem the party of Lincoln, Eisenhower and Reagan. And we will stand with them.

Yes, Trump will try to keep dominating and defining the Republican Party. But his power will wane when his name is no longer on the ballot. And no other candidate can unite and animate the unique base of support he has created.

The fight will be difficult. The MAGA movement will not go quietly. Victory is not at all certain. But the point is that there will be a fight. For the first time in a decade, traditional Republicans will have a fighting chance to take our party back.

So it is time to stand up. It is time for principled conservatives to re-engage. Get active in your party again. Run for party offices. And get ready to fight to win primary elections and caucuses in 2028 to nominate and elect the next great Republican president. 

Some say that our party will never come back; that the damage is permanent. Nonsense. Almost nothing in this life is permanent. And in politics, change comes with every election.

Traditional Republicans need to heed these words sometimes attributed to Winston Churchill, a great conservative who lost as many political battles as he won: “Success is not final, failure is not fatal: it is the courage to continue that counts.”

Chris Vance is a former chairman of the Washington State Republican Party and a former Washington State Representative. He was the state’s 2016 GOP nominee for U.S. Senate, and now serves as a senior advisor to Our Republican Legacy.

data.title: Live updates: Massive 'No Kings' rallies expected; DHS shutdown in stalemate as House passes its own bill
data.description:
Protesters around the country are joining “No Kings” rallies on Saturday, the third round since Trump returned to office.

Minnesota, home to two fatal shootings at the hands of federal officers earlier this year, will take center stage in the demonstrations, drawing even Bruce Springsteen. Organizers say more than 3,300 local events are planned nationwide. Here’s what to know about them.

The House on Friday night passed its own legislation to fund the Department of Homeland Security (DHS) in its entirety, as the partial government shutdown stretches into a seventh week.

Calling it “a joke,” House Speaker Mike Johnson pushed forward with a temporary stopgap measure rather than agreeing to put the Senate-passed DHS funding bill, passed unanimously in the wee hours of Friday. Both chambers have now left D.C. for a two-week recess. Here are takeaways from how it all unfolded.

Transportation Security Administration officers could still get paid as soon as Monday, however, after President Trump on Friday signed a memo telling DHS to cut paychecks anyway.

Pointing to such dysfunction, a record number of Congress members are leaving.

Follow along live.

data.title: Paul 'thinking about' 2028 White House bid
data.description:
Sen. Rand Paul (R-Ky.) indicated he is considering a presidential run in 2028 in a new interview clip released on Friday morning. 

“We’re thinking about it, and I would say 50-50,” the GOP lawmaker told CBS News’s Robert Costa, adding he will “make a decision” after this November’s midterm elections. 

Paul’s full interview with the network will air on Sunday on “CBS Sunday Morning.” 

The senator previously ran for president in 2016, before dropping out to run for reelection in the Senate. He has been a vocal critic of several of the Trump administration’s policies and previously said he would not back Vice President Vance in a presidential contest. 

Vance is widely considered to be a frontrunner for the Republican ticket in 2028, along with Secretary of State Marco Rubio, Florida Gov. Ron DeSantis and Texas Gov. Greg Abbott. 

Additionally, Paul has notably broken with his party on several legislative issues, recently voting with Democrats to support a war powers resolution to rein in President Trump’s military actions against Iran. 

Paul also refused to vote for the appointment of Trump’s newest pick to lead the Department of Homeland Security, Markwayne Mullin, after the pair clashed during Mullin’s confirmation hearing. The Senate confirmed Mullin’s appointment on Monday. 

The conservative legislator, who has run on libertarian values, said there is “not much left” in terms of the Republican Party’s “free market slash libertarian wing.”

“In fact, on many days it’s me in the Senate, the only one left for free trade,” he said, adding he still believes there’s a “desire” among voters in the business community for these values.

“If you combine [the libertarian vote] with the chamber of the commerce and the traditional business community that doesn’t like protectionism, there may be a force out there for a different direction for the party other than being continued to be led by populism,” he said.

data.title: Maher chides Trump over Mark Twain prize criticism: 'Keep the game going, baby'
data.description:
Comedian Bill Maher on Friday chided President Trump after it was announced that the HBO “Real Time” host will receive this year’s Mark Twain Prize for American Humor, telling critics to “keep the game going, baby.”

Maher told his audience he was “not looking for a fight” with the president. He added that he and Trump have a “complicated relationship” going back to when Trump sued the comedian for $5 million in 2013. Maher at the time joked that the then-businessman was “the spawn of his mother having sex with an orangutan.” Trump later dropped the suit.

“You know, this has been going on a long time,” Maher said Friday. “So him trying to block me from getting it –– I respect the move. Keep it going, baby. I just want things to work out, and also I want to say thank you, OK?”

Maher dismissed Trump’s clapbacks and urged the president to “get it off your chest, big man, OK? I’m totally fine with it.” He added that he was “honored” to accept the award and said he hopes Trump will attend the event in person before the Kennedy Center closes for two years for renovations.

“I will be there, Don, and I hope you will be too. I mean, the place is named after you now, you really should show up,” he said, referring to the center’s renaming to be called The Donald J. Trump and The John F. Kennedy Memorial Center for the Performing Arts.

“You could thank me for being one of the few people on the lunatic left who’s glad you hit Iran and is hoping we win that one, OK?” Maher said as his audience applauded and cheered.

The White House initially dismissed The Atlantic’s reporting that Maher would receive the award, with press secretary Karoline Leavitt calling it “fake news.” A White House official later told The Hill’s ITK that “the situation changed after further conversations took place between the Trump-Kennedy Center and event organizers over the past week.”

Maher has remained critical of the president but has delivered more praise since Trump’s return to the White House last year. Maher called Trump “gracious and measured” after meeting with him for dinner at the White House last year. The president later accused the comedian of “Trump derangement syndrome” over his account of the meeting, which Maher retorted was “bulls—.“

Trump critics slammed the meeting. “Seinfeld” co-creator Larry David penned a short story published in The New York Times about the meeting called “My Dinner with Adolf,” a fictional first-person account of a person having dinner with Nazi dictator Adolf Hitler. Maher called the piece “insulting” to Holocaust victims.

The administration’s takeover of the Kennedy Center was met with a wave of criticism, including from members of the Kennedy family. The venue has started the process of laying off employees ahead of its closure on July 4.

Demonstrations on Friday outside the center featured actor Jane Fonda and musician Joan Baez, who criticized what they said were threats to free speech during Trump’s second term.

data.title: A president at war needs resolute advice, not cheerleading
data.description:
How much does any incoming president truly understand about the horrors and unpredictability of war, and of the intelligence gathering behind the scenes that (hopefully) precedes it? In the lifetime of the most senior among us, only one president may have intuitively grasped those issues upon assuming the presidency: former World War II Supreme Allied Commander and five-star Gen. Dwight D. Eisenhower. 

Few presidents in recent decades have had any military experience. And there is nothing wrong with that, particularly as the draft was ended in December of 1972. But in a time of war, real-world military experience can be everything.   

Not since the end of the George H.W. Bush administration in 1992 have we had any commander in chief with combat experience. And without that experience, be they Bill Clinton, Geoge W. Bush, Barack Obama, Joe Biden or Donald Trump, that president is going to be completely dependent upon the military and intelligence experts at their disposal to make the proper real-time decisions leading up to “Go” or “No go.” 

It is exactly at those times that presidents need “adult in the room” thinking, and not cheerleaders.

No one understood that better than President Ronald Reagan. As he was about to be sworn in as president in January of 1981, he went against all conventional wisdom and advice, reaching out to James Baker — the man who had been campaign manager for both Gerald Ford and George H.W. Bush — to be his chief of staff. Said Baker upon reflection of that unexpected decision: “You could have picked me up off the floor with a blotter, that he would go to someone who managed two campaigns against him and make him his White House Chief. Probably will never happen again in American politics.” 

Reagan wanted to hear every side on every consequential issue before him. Donald Trump has been the same way, since he first entered the business world. 

I believe Trump has brought more real-world, commonsense experience into the Oval Office than any president in our lifetimes. Far too many have been career politicians and could not relate to the old Calvin Coolidge quote, “The business of America is business.” Trump can. But sometimes, the “business of America” morphs into war.   

As Vice President JD Vance so accurately conveyed about Trump when answering a question regarding Joe Kent’s public resignation in protest from the National Counterterrorism Center: “I know the president very well. He welcomes differences of opinion. He likes it when people express their views on what should happen. He listens to everybody. One of the great things I like about him is whether you’re the gardener at Mar-a-Lago or whether you’re secretary of State, the president cares about what you think about history. He recognizes that everybody has wisdom.”

“That said,” Vance continued, “whatever your view is, when the president of the U.S. makes a decision, it’s your job to help make that decision as effective and successful as possible.” 

Trump is a fighter. He fought to build a global business empire, and now, over the course of the five-plus years of his presidency, he has fought for the American people and our nation.      

The president is, by instinct, a very tough guy. The attacks, abuse and smears he has had to endure over the last decade — including an assassination attempt, where his very first reaction was to jump back up and yell, “fight, fight, fight” — have only made him tougher. Those who advise him on a daily basis must not only understand that reality, but factor it into the process.   

His instinct now is to fight for his country. But to do so, Trump needs to hear the good, the bad, and the ugly surrounding every policy or potential military operation. As former heavy-weight champion boxer Mike Tyson – who Trump knows well — so accurately said back in 1987: “Everyone has a plan until they get hit for the first time.” 

President Trump is a man very secure in his own skin. He does not need to be showered in compliments by advisers. If and when the U.S. gets hit, he needs to hear every single well-reasoned recommendation, be it optimistic or somber. That is how you serve a president always ready to wade into the fight for the American people. 

Douglas MacKinnon is a former White House and Pentagon official.
